174.reason: [Break after freeing only the first occurrence; multiple edges to del_num may exist but only one is removed]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-1: 0.25 , reason:  [Breaks after removing only the first occurrence; multiple edges to del_num may exist and not all are removed]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-2: 0.25 , reason:  [Only removes first occurrence of del_num in adjacency list; should remove all occurrences]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-3: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [Break after freeing only the first occurrence; multiple edges to del_num may exist but only one is removed]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-4: 0.2 , reason:  [Only removes the first occurrence of the deleted vertex in adjacency list. If multiple edges exist (shouldn't in simple graph), it fails. But more critically, after shifting vertices, other adjacencies > del_num are decremented, but this removal only handles exact match to del_num 鈥 which is correct, but the break causes only one edge to be removed even if duplicates existed (though input says no duplicates). However, the real issue is that after shifting G[m] = G[m+1], the links point to old indices, and the removal logic assumes original numbering. But the bigger flaw is in BFS/DFS using node labels as indices.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-5: 0.2 , reason:  [Only the first occurrence of the deleted vertex in the adjacency list is removed. If there are multiple edges (which shouldn't happen in a simple graph, but the logic is still flawed), it would miss others. However, more critically, after freeing 'now', the loop breaks, but if the deleted vertex appears again later (e.g., due to insertion order), it won't be removed. But the bigger issue is that the BFS/DFS use node values as indices incorrectly.]

81.reason: [The deQueue function returns the node value (G[v].node), but 'v' here is used as an index into G[]. This causes incorrect indexing because after deletion, G[v].node may not equal v.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-4: 0.2 , reason:  [The deQueue function returns the node value (G[v].node), but 'v' here is used as an index into G[]. This causes incorrect indexing because after deletion, G[v].node may not equal v.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-5: 0.2 , reason:  [The deQueue function returns the node value (G[v].node), but 'v' here is used as an index into G[]. This causes incorrect indexing because after deletion, G[] stores shifted vertices, and the node value may not match the array index.]

83.reason: [Using 'v' (which is a node label, not an array index) to access G[v] leads to out-of-bounds or wrong memory access.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-4: 0.2 , reason:  [Using 'v' (which is a node label, not an array index) to access G[v] leads to out-of-bounds or wrong memory access.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-5: 0.2 , reason:  [Using 'v' (which is a node label, not an index) to access G[v] is incorrect after vertex deletion because the graph structure has been compacted by shifting, so node labels no longer correspond to array indices.]
